journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Visualization of the interaction between sphingomyelin and cholesterol in lipid bilayer membranes
abstract: Biomembranes are complex two-dimensional liquids composed of hundreds of lipid species that interact in a myriad of ways. One such interaction, that between sphingomyelin (SM) and cholesterol in plasma membranes of animal cells, provides many functional benefits, including protection from microbial infection, prevention of unrestrained cell growth, and proper maintenance of cellular lipid composition. Owing to the liquid nature of membranes, the structure of the SM/cholesterol interaction, or any other functionally critical lipid–lipid interaction, has remained elusive. Here, we overcome this challenge using a fungal toxin called Ostreolysin A (OlyA), that has been shown to specifically bind to SM/cholesterol complexes in membranes. We used OlyA to stabilize the SM/cholesterol interaction much in the same way as antibodies are used to stabilize preexisting protein complexes. Cryoelectron microscopy analysis of OlyA bound to SM/cholesterol membranes reveals the details of the tight interaction between these two lipids—the steroid nucleus of cholesterol packs against the acyl chains of SM, and a hydrogen bond forms between the nitrogen on SM’s ceramide base and the oxygen on cholesterol’s hydroxyl group, thus sequestering this key functional group of cholesterol. The importance of hydrogen bonding in stabilizing the SM/cholesterol interaction is supported by structural analysis of a mutant form of OlyA that binds free SM in a cholesterol-independent manner. These results provide structural insights into the organization of cholesterol in membranes.
discussion: DiscussionMany earlier studies have proposed the idea of SM/cholesterol complexes in membranes , however despite countless efforts, such complexes have not been isolated or visualized . In the current study, cryoEM analysis of membrane-bound OlyA addresses this problem by providing insights into the details of the interaction between SM and cholesterol in a membrane (Fig. 3B and Movie S1). The steroid nucleus of cholesterol packs against the acyl chains of SM and their interaction is further stabilized through a hydrogen bond between the nitrogen on SM’s ceramide base and the oxygen on cholesterol’s hydroxyl group. The headgroup of SM arches over the hydroxyl group of cholesterol to sequester this moiety, a structural feature that provides a molecular basis for the inability of cholesterol-sensing proteins to detect this sequestered form of cholesterol . While our structure only shows a 1:1 SM:cholesterol interaction, we cannot rule out the possibility of other stoichiometries, such as 2:1 or 1:2, that have been suggested in earlier studies . Furthermore, even a 1:1 interaction could have several molecular orientations in liquid membranes, of which our structure is one example. Indeed, inasmuch as our structure was obtained with the aid of OlyA, the details of the interaction between SM and cholesterol in the absence of a stabilizing agent such as OlyA remain to be determined. Despite these limitations, we are hopeful that the atomic details of SM/cholesterol interactions provided in this study will be useful in advancing molecular models of membrane structure in animal cells as well as in membranes of fungi, plants, and bacteria that have analogs of SM and cholesterol (Fig. 4).Our results could also prove useful in understanding general principles of how lipid-sensing proteins sense their target lipids in a membrane. The current study focuses on the specific interactions between OlyA and an SM bound to cholesterol (Fig. 3), however the OlyA/membrane interaction footprint covers an area much larger than just one SM and one cholesterol. As such, we observed several other lipids bound to OlyA (Fig. 3 C–F). These lipids included two additional SMs and one additional cholesterol. Inasmuch as the lipid bilayer is a solvent for membrane-embedded or membrane-contacting proteins (such as OlyA), it is not surprising to find multiple bound solvent lipids, much like it is not surprising that crystal structures of a soluble protein such as trypsin reveal more than 100 bound water solvent molecules . However, the all-or-none specificity of OlyA for SM/cholesterol membranes (Fig. 1B) is driven by just one interaction, with the 1:1 SM/cholesterol complex, much like the hydrolytic cleavage activity of trypsin is driven by just one of the bound water molecules in its active site . In the structural view depicted in Fig. 3E, a free, accessible cholesterol (Chol2) is adjacent to a SM-sequestered cholesterol (Chol1). Also, free SMs (SM2 and SM3) are adjacent to an SM interacting with cholesterol (SM1). While we purposefully chose a liposomal system (1:1 SM:cholesterol) that does not undergo phase separation to simplify molecular interpretations, it is worth noting that studies of model membranes have shown that SM/cholesterol complexes can form in the absence of phase separations and be well-mixed in a single phase with free SM and free cholesterol molecules .In summary, our study provides answers to longstanding questions about how SM and cholesterol interact in membranes. The approaches described here for these two lipids could serve as a prototype to study interactions between other lipids and their respective sensor proteins.
figure_legends: Architecture of the membrane-spanning OlyA/PlyB pore complex. (A) Crystal structure of OlyA [Protein Data Bank (PDB):6MYJ]. (B) Binding of purified OlyA-His6 to liposomes. Binding to liposomes composed of either SM or equimolar amounts of the indicated lipids was measured by dot blot analysis as described in SI Appendix, Methods. Data points represent the mean ± SEM (n = 3). DOPC, di-oleoyl-phosphatidylcholine; Chol., cholesterol; Epichol., epicholesterol. (C) Chemical structure of lipids. Structural features of N-oleoyl-D-erythro-sphingosylphosphorylcholine (Sphingomyelin; SM) and cholesterol are labeled. (D) Schematic representation of the steps in OlyA/PlyB pore complex formation on SM/cholesterol membranes. OlyA and PlyB are colored blue and green, respectively. (E) Binding of purified His8-PlyB to SM/cholesterol liposomes. Binding to liposomes composed of equimolar amounts of SM and cholesterol, in the absence or presence of 1 µM (16 µg/mL) OlyA (whose His6-tag was removed by cleavage with TEV protease), was measured by dot blot analysis as described in SI Appendix, Methods. Data points represent the mean ± SEM (n = 3). (F) A representative cryoEM micrograph of the OlyA/PlyB pore complex on liposomes composed of equimolar amounts of SM and cholesterol (Top panel; Scale bar, 20 nm) and representative 2D class averages with side- and top–down views of the pore complex (Bottom panels). (G and H) CryoEM maps of the OlyA/PlyB pore complex bound to liposomal membranes composed of equimolar amounts of SM and cholesterol. Image processing was carried out as described in SI Appendix, Methods. Shown are a side-view of the full 13-subunit pore (G) and two subunits of the pore complex (focused refinement map, H). OlyA (blue), PlyB (green), extra densities around the membrane-spanning β-barrel of PlyB (pink).

Enhancing OlyA resolution at the membrane interface. (A) Comparison of solution and membrane-bound structures of PlyB. (Left) Crystal structure of the soluble form of PlyB (PDB: 4OEJ) highlighting key structural motifs. (Right) CryoEM map, after focused refinement, of the membrane-bound form of PlyB (from Fig. 1). Yellow and red spheres denote amino acid pairs that are within 7 Å in the solution form of PlyB and are separated by more than 100 Å in the membrane-bound form of PlyB. Pink spheres denote a pair of glycines that act as a hinge to facilitate the large conformational changes in PlyB upon membrane binding. (B) Comparison of the local resolution in unsharpened cryoEM maps of complexes of OlyA and three different versions of PlyB. All complexes contain the same version of OlyA complexed to PlyB [wild-type (WT)] (Left), PlyB (Hairpin Lock) (Middle), or PlyB (Hinge Lock) (Right). Local resolution estimations were calculated with cryoSPARC and are represented using a color-scale gradient with ChimeraX. (C) Comparison of OlyA bound to different versions of PlyB. Shown are cryoEM maps, after focused refinement, of the OlyA dimer from complexes with PlyB (WT) (Left), PlyB (Hairpin Lock) (Middle), or PlyB (Hinge Lock) (Right). The OlyA dimers are colored blue with membrane lipid densities colored pink.

Visualization of SM and cholesterol bound to OlyA. (A) CryoEM map, after low-pass filtering, of the OlyA/PlyB (Hinge Lock) complex showing connecting density between the membrane surface and dimer interface of OlyA (see rightmost panel of Fig. 2B for the original map). (B) CryoEM map, after focused refinement, showing a bound SM/cholesterol complex at the dimer interface of OlyA (derived from the rightmost panel of Fig. 2C). Map density for OlyA is displayed as a transparent surface over the model of OlyA (blue). Map density of the bound SM/cholesterol complex is displayed as a mesh surface with models of SM (light orange) and cholesterol (yellow). (C) CryoEM map, after focused refinement, of the OlyA/PlyB (Hinge Lock) complex showing the OlyA dimer (blue) and bound lipids at the membrane interface. Lipid densities for SM (light orange) and cholesterol (yellow) are shown as transparent surfaces over the lipid structural models. (D) Bottom-view (from the membrane bilayer) of the OlyA dimer with bound lipids. The dashed line denotes the dimer interface of OlyA. (E) Top-view of the OlyA dimer. For convenience, the structure of OlyA is omitted to show the organization of OlyA-bound lipids at the membrane interface. SM and cholesterol are displayed as transparent spheres over stick models. (F) Three conformations of SM. The lipid models are shown as spheres (transparency denotes parts of the modeled lipids lacking signal in the cryoEM maps). Oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus atoms are colored red, blue, and orange, respectively.

Hydrogen bonding stabilizes the interaction between SM and cholesterol. (A) Analogs of cholesterol and SM. (First column) The steroid nucleus of cholesterol and the ceramide backbone plus the amide-linked acyl chain of SM visualized in the interaction of SM and cholesterol (Fig. 3 and Movie S1) are highlighted in green. (Next three columns) Major plasma membrane lipids from plants, fungi, and bacteria containing a hydrocarbon ring system, like in the mammalian cholesterol, and an amide-linked acyl chain, like in the mammalian SM, are highlighted in yellow. Amide nitrogens and hydroxyl oxygens are colored blue and red, respectively. (B) Membrane-bound structure of OlyA (from Fig. 3) highlighting the location of residue E69. (C) Comparison of membrane binding properties of OlyA and OlyA (E69A). Binding to liposomes with the indicated compositions was measured by dot blot analysis as described in SI Appendix, Methods. Data points represent the mean ± SEM (n = 3). The mean binding value for 30 µg/mL of each version of OlyA to liposomes composed of 50% SM and 50% cholesterol was set to one and all other binding values were normalized to this set-point. Chol., cholesterol; Epi., epicholesterol. (D) Hydrogen bonding geometries. The experimentally determined SM/cholesterol interaction (Fig. 3 and Movie S1) was used as a template to align the steroid nucleus of epicholesterol in the same orientation. The distances between the amide nitrogen of SM and the hydroxyl oxygens of cholesterol and epicholesterol are 3.4 Å (dashed line) and 4.9 Å (solid line), respectively. (E) Comparison of lipid densities in structures of OlyA/PlyB (Hinge Lock) bound to SM/cholesterol membranes and OlyA (E69A)/PlyB (Hinge Lock) bound to SM/epicholesterol membranes. Both unsharpened focused refinement cryoEM maps are contoured at 12 σ. The densities are colored as follows—OlyA or OlyA (E69A) (blue), SM (light orange), cholesterol (yellow), other undefined (gray).
